title: Exploring Fabrice Muamba's Story and Writing Your Own
simple_explanation: This page helps you be a super smart reader by spotting facts, opinions, and special 'extra bits' of information in Fabrice's story. Then, it's your turn to be the author and write about a truly special moment from your own life, making sure your words are spot on!
detailed_explanation: You'll also practice identifying how the writer creates a clear picture of the setting and how he expresses his mixed feelings about the important football match. A key part of the questions is understanding 'parenthesis'. This is when extra information is added to a sentence using brackets, dashes, or commas, to give more detail without breaking the main flow. After being a story detective, you'll become a story writer! You'll write your own paragraph for an autobiography about a memorable moment in your life. When you write, try to use 'precise language' (exact words) and different sentence structures, including those with 'extra information' in parenthesis, and make sure your punctuation is perfect.

examples_expanded.text: A fact from the story is '17 March 2012'. An opinion is 'This is my chance' (because it's what he believes).
examples_expanded.context: This example shows how we use the concept of analyzing non-fiction: comprehension & writing in a real sentence.

examples_expanded.text: Setting details like 'The drizzle falls from the skies' and 'a full house at White Hart Lane' help you visualize the scene.
examples_expanded.context: This example shows how we use the concept of analyzing non-fiction: comprehension & writing in a real sentence.

examples_expanded.text: For parenthesis, look at the example: 'If anyone wants anything doing – for the team or for a mate or whoever – then I'll happily get on and do it.' The part between the dashes is the extra information.
examples_expanded.context: This example shows how we use the concept of analyzing non-fiction: comprehension & writing in a real sentence.

examples_expanded.text: When writing your own paragraph, you could describe your special moment, like 'winning the school talent show (even though I was so nervous!)' to add extra detail.
examples_expanded.context: This example shows how we use the concept of analyzing non-fiction: comprehension & writing in a real sentence.

guided_practice: Identify the meaning of 'fact' in a sentence. Remember it means: Something that is known to be true or to have happened.
Match the word 'opinion' with a similar concept from your own experience.
Look at the first example again. How would you modify it to make it your own?
application_tasks: Create 3 of your own sentences using the core idea of Analyzing Non-fiction: Comprehension & Writing.
Imagine you are teaching this topic to a friend. Write down exactly what you would say.
challenge_tasks: Combine the concept of 'fact' with something you learned in a previous chapter to write a short paragraph.
Can you find an exception to this rule or a creative way to bend it? Explain how.
key_points: Distinguish between facts and opinions in non-fiction texts.
Analyze how authors convey feelings and describe settings.
Understand and identify parenthesis as a way to add extra information.
Practice writing your own autobiographical paragraph using precise language and varied punctuation.

vocabulary.definition: Something that is known to be true or to have happened.
A belief or judgment about something, not necessarily based on fact.
The time and place where a story happens.
To give a detailed account of something in words.
The state or fact of being of great significance or value.
A group of words taken from a text or speech and repeated by someone else.
An explanatory or qualifying word, clause, or sentence inserted into a passage, often marked by brackets, dashes, or commas.
A true story of a person's life, written by that person.
Worth remembering or easily remembered, because of being special or unusual.
Using exact and careful words to convey meaning clearly.
A group of words that acts like an adverb, telling us how, when, where, or why something happened.
Words that name a person, place, thing, or idea.
Words that describe nouns.
Words that describe an action, state, or occurrence.
Additional details that clarify or expand on a point.
Words like 'who', 'which', 'that', 'whose', 'whom' used to introduce clauses that give more information about a noun.
Punctuation marks ( ) or [ ] used to enclose extra information.
Punctuation marks (–) used to set off extra information or create a pause.
Punctuation marks (,) used to separate items in a list, or clauses in a sentence.
A punctuation mark (;) used to join two closely related independent clauses or to separate items in a complex list.
A punctuation mark (:) used to introduce a list, an explanation, or a quotation.
